title: Beyond Brexit—and back to Europe – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Ten years after the Brexit vote and amid PM Keir Starmer's resignation, ECFR polling shows a quiet revolution in UK and EU public opinion: 75% of Britons want a closer relationship with Europe and rejoining the EU is the most popular option, driven largely by Trump's return making the US an unreliable partner. Mark Leonard argues the political class has not caught up with voters and Labour may put EU membership back on the agenda.

insights.claim: A major study puts the cumulative cost of Brexit at 6-8% of the UK's GDP over the decade since the 2016 referendum.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Quantifies a persistent structural drag on UK growth assets and sterling versus EU-exposed peers; a re-engagement trajectory would be a long-term GBP and UK-equity catalyst.
insights.quote: Just last week, a major study put the cost of Brexit at 6-8% of the UK’s GDP over the decade since the vote.
insights.locator: Starmer is leaving with characteristic dignity

insights.claim: Britain's withdrawal from the EU was a key reason Keir Starmer failed to deliver his promised revival of the British economy, contributing to his resignation.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Ties UK political instability directly to the EU relationship, signalling Europe policy as the pivotal variable for the next government's economic credibility.
insights.quote: One key reason for his failure was Britain’s withdrawal from the EU.
insights.locator: Starmer is leaving with characteristic dignity

insights.claim: Despite a dramatic shift in UK and EU public opinion, the intergovernmental and media debate remains frozen, with London still invoking previous governments' Europe 'red lines' and Brussels resisting UK 'cherry picking'.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: The gap between voters and policymakers is the tradable mispricing: re-engagement is more likely than elite rhetoric implies, an asymmetric upside for UK-EU integration plays.
insights.quote: while the elite debate remains the same, public opinion on both sides of the English Channel has undergone a remarkable shift
insights.locator: But while the elite debate remains the same

insights.claim: Many EU politicians still frame Brexit as a 'bad divorce' and harbour a desire to 'punish the UK', and are wary of putting their fate in the hands of Britain's fickle electorate again.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: EU-side resentment is a real friction that could delay or dilute any rapprochement even as publics align; watch Commission red-line rhetoric as the leading indicator.
insights.quote: They harbour resentments against those Britons who always saw the EU as a market rather than a political community.

insights.claim: Nigel Farage's Reform party is leading the polls in the UK even as public opinion shifts toward Europe.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A pro-Europe electorate co-existing with a poll-leading Eurosceptic party signals volatile, realigning UK politics—elevated political-risk premium on UK assets near elections.
insights.quote: Nigel Farage’s Reform party is leading the polls in the UK
insights.locator: To be sure, you wouldn’t know it

insights.claim: An ECFR poll finds 75% of Britons want a closer relationship with Europe, and rejoining the EU is the single most popular option when asked to define that relationship.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A supermajority for closer ties makes a future rejoin referendum a plausible multi-year scenario, reshaping the UK regulatory and trade outlook for sterling and gilts.
insights.quote: Some 75% of Britons want a closer relationship with Europe, and when asked to define what that means, the most popular choice is rejoining the EU
insights.locator: Some 75% of Britons want a closer relationship

insights.claim: The desire to re-engage with Europe cuts across the old leave-remain divide, with six out of ten 'leave' voters now open to a closer relationship.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Erosion of the leave bloc removes the core political constraint that blocked re-engagement, raising the base rate for pro-EU policy through the 2030s.
insights.quote: six out of ten “leave” voters expressing openness to a closer relationship
insights.locator: The desire to re-engage even cuts across

insights.claim: Donald Trump's return to office is the biggest single driver of the UK's swing toward Europe, with large majorities of UK voters now looking to Europe rather than America for economic, security, and border protection.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: US unreliability is structurally pushing the UK and EU together—a durable tailwind for European defence integration and intra-European trade/security arrangements.
insights.quote: The biggest gamechanger has been Donald Trump’s return to office.

insights.claim: EU publics increasingly see Trump, Putin, and Xi as having changed the world for the worse, and are reframing a new cross-channel relationship with the UK around their own security interests rather than punishing Britain.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A security-first rationale (vs trade) lowers the political bar for UK-EU defence cooperation, favouring European defence and dual-use industrial exposure.
insights.quote: they are asking how a new cross-channel relationship could serve their own security interests

insights.claim: Europeans fear US disengagement combined with China threatening 'de-industrialisation and strategic subordination' is forcing them to plan for self-defence.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Twin US-withdrawal and China-pressure narrative underpins sustained European defence and reshoring spend—a multi-year fiscal and industrial theme.
insights.quote: China is threatening them with de-industrialisation and strategic subordination
insights.locator: They are thinking about what it will take

insights.claim: The Europe relationship has flipped from a 2016 fault line that split Labour into one of the few issues that now unites the old Labour coalition.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A unifying Europe stance gives Labour electoral incentive to move past a timid 'reset', making bolder UK-EU policy a credible governing path.
insights.quote: now, the relationship with Europe is one of the few things that unites the old Labour coalition
insights.locator: The 2016 Brexit vote created a crisis

insights.claim: Nine in ten Labour-to-Green defectors and eight in ten Labour-to-Lib-Dem defectors want to rejoin the EU, and even a plurality of Labour-to-Reform defectors would vote to rejoin today.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Rejoin sentiment even among Reform-leaning defectors shows the issue can rebuild Labour's coalition, increasing odds EU membership returns to the formal agenda.
insights.quote: Even a plurality of the voters who left Labour for the far-right Reform party (the successor to the old Brexit party) say they would vote to rejoin the EU were a referendum held today.
insights.locator: Nine out of ten voters who left Labour

insights.claim: Voter demand for a 'fundamentally different option' than a mere 'reset' may push Labour to put EU membership back on the agenda.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: An explicit membership debate within Labour would be a regime-change catalyst for UK assets; monitor Labour leadership signals post-Starmer.
insights.quote: this demand may lead Labour to put EU membership back on the agenda
insights.locator: That is why the big debate within Labour

insights.claim: From Poland to Portugal, majorities in every country polled would like Britain to rejoin, and many believe the European Commission should soften its own red lines, especially on security.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Continent-wide public appetite for UK return gives political cover for the Commission to ease terms—watch Brussels' red-line language as the rapprochement leading indicator.
insights.quote: From Poland to Portugal, majorities in every country polled would like Britain to rejoin

insights.claim: Leonard frames the current moment as a chance for European leaders like Macron to make undoing Brexit a 'historic achievement' for their legacies.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Legacy-seeking by senior EU leaders is a soft catalyst that could accelerate a UK-EU deal independent of bureaucratic inertia.
insights.quote: undoing the damage of Brexit would be a historic achievement
insights.locator: And as European leaders like French president
